refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. The p95 metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions. Refinement clarifies that this is an end-to-end requirement spanning network, vehicle, and control systems.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure round-trip latency (command transmission + vehicle response + telemetry return) under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Collect p95 percentile across 1000+ operations. Validate against 150 ms threshold with safety margin analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire vehicle state and sensor data with sufficient frequency to support 150 ms end-to-end latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands to the vehicle with latency contribution ≤ 50 ms (allocated portion of 150 ms budget).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and process vehicle telemetry with latency contribution ≤ 50 ms (allocated portion of 150 ms budget).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall manage network link quality and gracefully degrade operation if latency exceeds safe thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency network protocol (UDP-based, minimal serialization) with local vehicle control loop and predictive telemetry buffering to reduce round-trip latency.
DP-B: Implement high-frequency sensor polling (100+ Hz) on vehicle with edge processing and delta-encoding to minimize telemetry payload and transmission time.
DP-C: Implement dual-path communication (primary + redundant) with automatic failover and latency monitoring to ensure consistent p95 performance.
DP-D: Implement adaptive bitrate and frame-rate control on telemetry stream based on network link quality to maintain latency under variable conditions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A + DP-B + DP-D (integrated approach): Establish low-latency UDP-based protocol with high-frequency vehicle sensor polling and edge processing, combined with adaptive telemetry streaming that adjusts bitrate/frame-rate based on real-time network link quality monitoring. This addresses latency at protocol, vehicle, and network adaptation layers.

potential_sources: Coupling between vehicle sensor polling frequency (DP-B) and network bandwidth availability (DP-D) — mitigated by adaptive bitrate control.
Coupling between UDP protocol choice (DP-A) and reliability requirements — mitigated by application-level acknowledgment and retransmission for critical commands.
Coupling between edge processing (DP-B) and vehicle compute capacity — acceptable if vehicle has sufficient CPU; requires validation.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP combination exhibits acceptable coupling. Adaptive telemetry (DP-D) decouples sensor frequency from network constraints. UDP protocol (DP-A) is standard for low-latency systems. Edge processing (DP-B) is localized to vehicle and does not create system-wide dependencies.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Vehicle has sufficient onboard compute capacity (CPU, memory) to support 100+ Hz sensor polling and edge processing without degrading vehicle control performance.
ASSUMPTION: Network link quality can be reliably measured and reported in real-time to enable adaptive bitrate decisions.
RISK: UDP packet loss under poor network conditions may require application-level retransmission, increasing latency variance and potentially exceeding p95 threshold.
RISK: Adaptive bitrate reduction may degrade operator situational awareness if telemetry quality drops below acceptable levels.
RISK: Edge processing logic errors or delays on vehicle could introduce latency spikes that violate p95 requirement.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Validate vehicle compute capacity and network measurement accuracy early in design. Establish clear thresholds for telemetry quality degradation and operator notification. Consider hybrid approach: UDP for low-latency commands, TCP for critical state updates with timeout-based fallback.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all teleoperation-specific logic: command reception from tele-operator, vehicle state acquisition, telemetry streaming, network protocol handling, latency monitoring, and adaptive quality control. Owns the end-to-end latency budget and is responsible for meeting p95 ≤ 150 ms.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from tele-operator interface. Latency-critical path.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and status to tele-operator display. Adaptive bitrate/frame-rate controlled based on network link quality.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Manages underlying network transport (UDP, TCP, or hybrid). Monitors link quality (latency, jitter, packet loss) and reports to adaptive control logic.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals safety-critical events (latency threshold exceeded, network link failure, vehicle fault) to Safety Services for operator notification and emergency stop.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the only one (Vehicle, Safety Services are peers). Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse teleoperation logic across multiple layers and obscure ownership. Not recommended.

Vehicle module owns vehicle-side sensor polling and edge processing (DP-B). However, teleoperation also includes tele-operator command reception, telemetry streaming, and network protocol handling, which are not vehicle-resident. Assigning entire teleoperation to Vehicle would create diffusion of operator-side logic. Partial containment only.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for this requirement. It naturally contains command reception, telemetry streaming, network protocol, latency monitoring, and adaptive quality control. Vehicle-side sensor polling (DP-B) is a dependency, not a containment issue. Excellent fit.

Safety Services owns safety-critical decision logic and alerts. Teleoperation latency monitoring is a safety input but not a safety decision. Assigning teleoperation to Safety Services would conflate operational and safety concerns. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of CR-NGHE-008. It encapsulates the end-to-end latency requirement, owns the network protocol and adaptive quality control logic, and interfaces with Vehicle (for sensor data) and Safety Services (for alerts). Containment score 0.95 reflects strong alignment.
containment_risks: RISK: Vehicle-side sensor polling frequency (DP-B) is owned by Vehicle module, not Teleoperation. Teleoperation must coordinate with Vehicle to ensure polling frequency supports latency budget. Requires clear interface contract.

RISK: Network Link quality measurement and reporting (DP-D) may be owned by a separate network/communication module. Teleoperation must depend on reliable link quality data. Requires interface clarity.
RISK: Safety alert generation (SafetyAlert interface) is owned by Safety Services. Teleoperation must signal latency violations promptly. Requires low-latency alert path.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit interface contracts: (1) Vehicle provides sensor data at ≥100 Hz via Telemetry interface; (2) Network Link provides real-time quality metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss) to Teleoperation; (3) Teleoperation signals latency threshold violations via SafetyAlert to Safety Services within 10 ms. Document latency budget allocation: Teleoperation owns 100 ms of 150 ms budget (command 50 ms + telemetry 50 ms), Vehicle owns 30 ms, Network Link owns 20 ms.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP (low-latency UDP protocol + high-frequency sensor polling + adaptive telemetry) directly addresses FR requirement for p95 ≤ 150 ms latency. DP is specific, measurable, and traceable to FR. No functional gaps identified.

diffusion_risks: Vehicle-side sensor polling (DP-B) is partially owned by Vehicle module, not Teleoperation. Mitigation: Clear interface contract specifying polling frequency and data format.
Network Link quality measurement (DP-D) may be owned by separate network module. Mitigation: Teleoperation depends on Network Link interface for quality metrics.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. Primary dependencies are Vehicle (sensor data) and Network Link (quality metrics), both external. Ownership is clear and diffusion is minimal. DP-to-IM mapping is sound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via end-to-end latency measurement under representative network conditions. Teleoperation module owns the measurement and reporting logic. p95 metric is quantifiable and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Latency budget allocation is clear: Teleoperation 100 ms (command 50 ms + telemetry 50 ms), Vehicle 30 ms, Network Link 20 ms. Each module has defined responsibility. Allocation is traceable to FR.

recommended_revisions: Formalize latency budget allocation in design specification with clear ownership per module.
Establish interface contracts for Vehicle sensor polling frequency and Network Link quality metrics.
Define SafetyAlert latency requirement (e.g., ≤10 ms) to ensure safety-critical notifications are timely.
Plan early validation of vehicle compute capacity to support DP-B (100+ Hz polling + edge processing).

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is not atomic. Refined FR identifies four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) that require further decomposition. Each sub-FR addresses a distinct aspect of the latency requirement: sensor acquisition, command transmission, telemetry reception, and network adaptation. Decomposition is necessary to allocate latency budget, assign ownership, and enable detailed design. FR-DP-IM mapping at current level is sound and gates are passed, but functional decomposition must continue to sub-FR level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire vehicle state and sensor data with sufficient frequency to support 150 ms end-to-end latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands to the vehicle with latency contribution ≤ 50 ms (allocated portion of 150 ms budget).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and process vehicle telemetry with latency contribution ≤ 50 ms (allocated portion of 150 ms budget).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall manage network link quality and gracefully degrade operation if latency exceeds safe thresholds.

assumptions: Vehicle has sufficient onboard compute capacity (CPU, memory) to support 100+ Hz sensor polling and edge processing.
Network link quality can be reliably measured and reported in real-time.
Tele-operator interface can receive and display telemetry at 50+ Hz without additional latency.
Safety Services module can receive and process SafetyAlert signals within 10 ms.
Agreed network conditions are well-defined and representative of operational scenarios.
open_risks: UDP packet loss under poor network conditions may increase latency variance and violate p95 threshold.
Vehicle compute capacity may be insufficient for DP-B (100+ Hz polling + edge processing), requiring design trade-off.
Adaptive telemetry bitrate reduction may degrade operator situational awareness below acceptable levels.
Edge processing logic errors or delays on vehicle could introduce latency spikes.
Network Link quality measurement accuracy and timeliness may be insufficient for adaptive control.
evidence_needed: Vehicle compute capacity analysis and benchmarking (CPU, memory, I/O bandwidth).
Network link quality measurement methodology and accuracy validation.
End-to-end latency measurement under representative network conditions (baseline and stress scenarios).
Operator situational awareness study to define minimum telemetry quality thresholds.
Safety analysis of latency threshold violations and emergency stop procedures.
Interface specifications for Vehicle (sensor data), Network Link (quality metrics), and Safety Services (alerts).
